5. Working with Files and Folders

When working with data, file organization matters.

If your files are organized, R can find them more easily.

What is a Working Directory?

The working directory is the folder where R is currently looking for files.

If you ask R to open a file, R first looks in the working directory.

For example:

read.csv("mydata.csv")

This tells R:

Look in the current working directory for a file called mydata.csv.

Checking Your Working Directory

You can check your working directory with:

getwd()
[1] "/Users/ylim2/Documents/01_Work/01_MA213/R_onboarding/R_onboarding_repo/R_Onboarding"

This prints the folder where R is currently working.

TipTip

If R cannot find your file, checking getwd() is often the first thing to do.

Creating a Project Folder

For this guide, create a folder called:

r-onboarding-guide

Inside that folder, you might have:

r-onboarding-guide/
├── first_script.R
├── first_report.qmd
└── mydata.csv

Why Use a Project Folder?

A project folder keeps related files together.

This helps you avoid problems like:

  • Losing your dataset.
  • Saving files in random places.
  • Not knowing which version of a file is correct.
  • Getting file not found errors.

Finding the Project Folder

In RStudio, use the Files tab to find your project folder.

[Insert Screenshot: Files Pane Showing Project Folder]

Show the Files tab with the project folder open.

Recommended File Organization

For a larger project, you may want folders like this:

my-project/
├── data/
├── scripts/
├── reports/
└── images/

For now, it is okay to keep things simple.

File Names Matter

Good file names are short and clear.

Good examples:

mydata.csv
first_script.R
lab_report.qmd

Less helpful examples:

Untitled1.R
final final data copy.csv
my data file.csv
TipTip

Avoid spaces in file names.

Use underscores _ instead:

my_data.csv
lab_report.qmd

Try It Yourself

NotePractice
  1. Create a folder for this course.
  2. Inside it, create a folder for this assignment or lab.
  3. Save your R script in that folder.
  4. In RStudio, run:
getwd()
  1. Check whether R is working in the folder you expected.

Common Mistake

WarningWarning

Avoid saving course files only in your Downloads folder.

The Downloads folder can become messy quickly, and files may be hard to find later.

Check Your Understanding

Answer these questions:

  1. What is a working directory?
  2. Why is it helpful to keep your script and data file in the same folder?
  3. What command shows the current working directory?
  4. Why should you avoid unclear file names?

Summary

In this section, you learned that:

  • The working directory is where R looks for files.
  • getwd() shows your current working directory.
  • A project folder keeps your work organized.
  • Clear file names help prevent mistakes. `````
